## Account
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    '001' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(kunden_nr), '[^0-9]', '', 'g'), 15, '0') AS "Id",
    COALESCE(NULLIF(INITCAP(TRIM(firmenname)), ''), 'Unknown Customer') AS "Name",
    TRIM(erp_nummer) AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE UPPER(TRIM(kategorie))
        WHEN 'GOLD' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN 'SILVER' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN 'BRONZE' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN 'PLATINUM' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM(gebiet) AS "Region__c",
    INITCAP(TRIM(branche)) AS "Industry",
    CASE
        WHEN webseite IS NULL OR TRIM(webseite) = '' THEN NULL
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(webseite)) LIKE 'http%' THEN
            'https://' || SUBSTR(TRIM(webseite), POSITION('//' IN TRIM(webseite)) + 2)
        ELSE 'https://' || TRIM(webseite)
    END AS "Website",
    INITCAP(TRIM(ort)) AS "BillingCity",
    CASE UPPER(TRIM(land))
        WHEN 'DEUTSCHLAND' THEN 'DE'
        WHEN 'ÖSTERREICH' THEN 'AT'
        WHEN 'SCHWEIZ' THEN 'CH'
        WHEN 'ITALIEN' THEN 'IT'
        WHEN 'FRANKREICH' THEN 'FR'
        WHEN 'SPANIEN' THEN 'ES'
        WHEN 'PORTUGAL' THEN 'PT'
        WHEN 'NIEDERLANDE' THEN 'NL'
        WHEN 'BELGIEN' THEN 'BE'
        WHEN 'LUXEMBURG' THEN 'LU'
        WHEN 'DÄNEMARK' THEN 'DK'
        WHEN 'SCHWEDEN' THEN 'SE'
        WHEN 'NORWEGEN' THEN 'NO'
        WHEN 'FINNLAND' THEN 'FI'
        WHEN 'POLSEN' THEN 'PL'
        WHEN 'POLEN' THEN 'PL'
        WHEN 'TSCHECHIEN' THEN 'CZ'
        WHEN 'UNGARN' THEN 'HU'
        WHEN 'RUMÄNIEN' THEN 'RO'
        WHEN 'BULGARIEN' THEN 'BG'
        WHEN 'GREICHENLAND' THEN 'GR'
        WHEN 'GREECE' THEN 'GR'
        WHEN 'VEREINIGTES KÖNIGREICH' THEN 'GB'
        WHEN 'IRLAND' THEN 'IE'
        WHEN 'ISLAND' THEN 'IS'
        WHEN 'LITAUEN' THEN 'LT'
        WHEN 'LETTLAND' THEN 'LV'
        WHEN 'ESTLAND' THEN 'EE'
        WHEN 'KROATIEN' THEN 'HR'
        WHEN 'SERBIEN' THEN 'RS'
        WHEN 'BOSNIEN UND HERZEGOWINA' THEN 'BA'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "BillingCountry",
    TRIM(kunden_nr) AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    CAST(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS TEXT) AS "CreatedDate",
    CAST(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS TEXT) AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'kunden') }}
```
Column notes:
  - Id <- kunden_nr: Transform kunden_nr (e.g. CUST-1001) to Salesforce-style ID by extracting digits and prefixing with 001, LPAD to 15 chars
  - Name <- firmenname: INITCAP trimmed company name with fallback to Unknown Customer if empty/missing
  - ERP_Number__c <- erp_nummer: Trimmed ERP number from source, NULL if missing
  - Customer_Tier__c <- kategorie: Map kategorie values (Gold/Silver/Bronze/Platinum) directly to enum domain
  - Region__c <- gebiet: Trimmed region/gebiet field from source
  - Industry <- branche: INITCAP trimmed industry/branche field
  - Website <- webseite: Normalize protocol to https://, lowercase domain portion
  - BillingCity <- ort: INITCAP trimmed city from ort field
  - BillingCountry <- land: Map German country names (e.g. DEUTSCHLAND, ITALIEN) to ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 codes via CASE; NULL for unmapped
  - Legacy_Customer_ID__c <- kunden_nr: Trimmed source natural key (CUST-XXXX format)

    CASE

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(ap.sprache)) IN ('ES', 'SPANISH', 'ESPANOL') THEN 'ES'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(ap.sprache)) IN ('IT', 'ITALIAN', 'ITALIANO') THEN 'IT'

    END AS "Preferred_Language__c",

## Installed_Asset__c
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT 
    -- Id: Transform asset_id to Salesforce Asset ID format (00u prefix + 15 digit number)
    '00u' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(a.asset_id), '[^0-9]', '', 'g'), 15, '0') AS "Id",

    -- Name: bezeichnung with INITCAP and TRIM, fallback to 'Unnamed Asset' if NULL/empty
    COALESCE(NULLIF(INITCAP(TRIM(a.bezeichnung)), ''), 'Unnamed Asset') AS "Name",

    -- Serial_Number__c: raw serial number with trim
    TRIM(a.seriennr) AS "Serial_Number__c",

    -- Warranty_End_Date__c: parse multiple date formats to ISO YYYY-MM-DD, NULL if missing/unparseable
    CASE 
        WHEN a.garantie_bis ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN a.garantie_bis::DATE::TEXT
        WHEN a.garantie_bis ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(a.garantie_bis, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN a.garantie_bis ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(a.garantie_bis, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Warranty_End_Date__c",

    -- Account__c: join with kunden and transform to Salesforce Account ID (001 prefix)
    CASE 
        WHEN TRIM(k.kunden_nr) IS NOT NULL THEN
            '001' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(k.kunden_nr), '[^0-9]', '', 'g'), 15, '0')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Account__c",

    -- Project__c: join with proj and transform to Salesforce Project ID (00P prefix)
    CASE 
        WHEN TRIM(p.proj_id) IS NOT NULL THEN
            '00P' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(p.proj_id), '[^0-9]', '', 'g'), 15, '0')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project__c",

    -- Legacy_Asset_ID__c: original natural key for row-level traceability
    TRIM(a.asset_id) AS "Legacy_Asset_ID__c",

    -- Audit fields (defaults since not in source)
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'assets') }} a
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'kunden') }} k 
    ON TRIM(a.kd_ref) = k.kunden_nr
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'proj') }} p 
    ON TRIM(a.projekt_ref) = TRIM(p.proj_id)

    -- Name: INITCAP(TRIM(bezeichnung)) with fallback for NULLs
    COALESCE(INITCAP(TRIM(c.bezeichnung)), 'Unnamed Opportunity') AS "Name",

    -- StageName: Map source phase (DE/EN) to SFDC stage enum — fixed syntax: each WHEN must be a single value
    CASE UPPER(TRIM(c.phase))
        WHEN 'CLOSED WON' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN 'CLOSED LOST' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN 'PROSPECTING' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN 'QUALIFICATION' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN 'NEEDS ANALYSIS' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN 'VALUE PROPOSITION' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN 'VALUE PROP' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN 'IDENTIFY DECISION MAKERS' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN 'ID. DECISION MAKERS' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN 'PERCEPTION ANALYSIS' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN 'PROPOSAL/PRICE QUOTE' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN 'PROPOSAL / PRICE QUOTE' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN 'NEGOTIATION/REVIEW' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN 'NEGOTIATION / REVIEW' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",

    -- CloseDate: Parse multiple text date formats to ISO YYYY-MM-DD string; NULL if unparseable
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(c.abschlussdatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TRIM(c.abschlussdatum)
        WHEN TRIM(c.abschlussdatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(c.abschlussdatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN TRIM(c.abschlussdatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN
            SUBSTR(TRIM(c.abschlussdatum), 1, 4) || '-' ||
            SUBSTR(TRIM(c.abschlussdatum), 5, 2) || '-' ||
            SUBSTR(TRIM(c.abschlussdatum), 7, 2)
        WHEN TRIM(c.abschlussdatum) ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(c.abschlussdatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",

    -- Amount: Cast double precision with fallback to 0.0 for missing values
    COALESCE(c.volumen, 0.0) AS "Amount",

    -- CurrencyIsoCode: Map source waehrung to ISO 4217 code; fixed syntax (multiple WHEN lines)
    CASE UPPER(TRIM(c.waehrung))
        WHEN 'EUR' THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN 'EURO' THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN 'USD' THEN 'USD'
        WHEN 'US-DOLLAR' THEN 'USD'
        WHEN 'DOLLAR' THEN 'USD'
        WHEN 'GBP' THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN 'POUND STERLING' THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN 'POUND' THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN 'CHF' THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN 'SWISS FRANC' THEN 'CHF'
        ELSE 'EUR'
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",

    -- AccountId: Transform kunden.kunden_nr to Salesforce Account ID format (prefix '001')
    '001' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(k.kunden_nr), '[^0-9]', ''), 15, '0') AS "AccountId",

    -- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c: Direct copy of source natural key
    TRIM(c.chance_id) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",

    -- Audit fields (not in source — deterministic defaults)
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'chancen') }} c
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'kunden') }} k
    ON TRIM(c.kd_nr) = TRIM(k.kunden_nr)

Reasoning: The database error was caused by invalid PostgreSQL CASE syntax: `WHEN 'VALUE PROPOSITION' OR 'VALUE PROP' THEN ...` is rejected because OR in a WHEN clause must evaluate to a boolean, not act as an alternative value list. The fix is to either split them into separate WHEN lines or use `CASE WHEN expr IN (...)`. Each value now gets its own WHEN line for clarity and correctness. Same fix applied to CurrencyIsoCode mapping which had identical syntax errors.

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'IN PLANNING' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'ON HOLD' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.status)) = 'CANCELLED' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE 'In Planning'
    END AS "Project_Status__c",

    -- Go_Live_Date__c: parse dates (already YYYY-MM-DD in source, but handle other formats)
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live IS NULL OR TRIM(p.go_live) = '' THEN NULL

        WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN
            SUBSTRING(p.go_live, 1, 4) || '-' ||
            SUBSTRING(p.go_live, 5, 2) || '-' ||
            SUBSTRING(p.go_live, 7, 2)
        ELSE NULL

Reasoning: ## Cross-Table Key & Join Strategy
- **Master Customer Table**: `kunden` is the system of record for Accounts. Natural key: `kunden.kunden_nr`.
- **Foreign Key Resolution**: All FK references to Account (`AccountId`, `Account__c`) join on the normalized source customer key (`kunde`, `kd_nr`, `kd`, `kd_ref`). Downstream must transform these natural keys into 18-character Salesforce-style IDs (e.g., prefix `001` + zero-padding or dedicated ID-gen macro). Do not pass raw source keys directly into `AccountId`/`Account__c`.
- **Opportunity FK**: `proj.opp` joins to `chancen.chance_id`. Same normalization/ID-generation rules apply.
- **Project FK**: `assets.projekt_ref` joins to `proj.proj_id`. Standardize casing/trimming before join.
- **Consistent Enum Fallback Policy**: Where source data is missing, malformed, or falls outside declared domains, output `NULL` unless the target column is `NOT NULL`, in which case use a sensible default (e.g., `'In Planning'` for statuses, `'Unknown'` for names).
- **Date Normalization**: All date columns (`abschlussdatum`, `go_live`, `garantie_bis`) support mixed formats: `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`. Parse explicitly using layered `TO_DATE`/regex guards; cast to ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` string. Missing/unparseable → `NULL`.
- **Amount/Currency Normalization**: European locale handling applied. Strip currency symbols (`€`, `$`, etc.) and text prefixes. Replace thousand-separator dots (`.`) with empty string, then replace decimal comma (`,`) with period (`.`). Cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Missing → `0.0` for numeric amounts.

## Cross-Cutting Transform Notes
- **Text Normalization**: All name/label columns use `INITCAP(TRIM())` for consistency. Emails: `LOWER(TRIM())`.
- **Legacy Columns**: Every `Legacy_*__c` column is populated directly from the source natural key to enable row-level traceability. No transformation other than `TRIM()`.

1. `Id` column has values like "0060000000PP-00001" - clearly wrong. The REGEXP_REPLACE is not working as expected in this context, leaving non-digit characters in the ID. It should be like "006000000000001".

The problem is that the Opportunity model uses:
```sql
'006' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(c.chance_id), '[^0-9]', ''), 15, '0') AS "Id",
```

Without the global flag `'g'`, and possibly with other issues. But wait, I tested earlier and REGEXP_REPLACE('OPP-00001', '[^0-9]', '') returned '00001' correctly when tested alone... but in the context of the full query it seems to be returning something else.

Let me check what's actually happening. Actually, looking at the output "0060000000PP-00001":
- If REGEXP_REPLACE returned 'PP-00001', then LPAD('PP-00001', 15, '0') = '0000000PP-00001' (9 chars + 6 zeros = 15)
- Then '006' + '0000000PP-00001' = '0060000000PP-00001' ✓

So the issue is that REGEXP_REPLACE('OPP-00001', '[^0-9]', '') is returning 'PP-00001' instead of '00001'. That means the 'O' and the first 'P' are somehow not being matched by `[^0-9]`. But that doesn't make sense...

Wait, let me look at this differently. Maybe there's something about the actual data in the source table that's different from what I saw in my sample query. Or maybe the regex is being interpreted differently in certain contexts.

Actually, I think I need to just accept that the Opportunity model has a bug where the ID generation is not working correctly. The fix would be to use a consistent regex pattern like `[^0-9]` with the global flag 'g', similar to how the Account model does it.

But wait - looking at the Contact model, it uses:
```sql
'002' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(ap.ap_id), '^[A-Za-z-]+', ''), 15, '0') AS "Id",
```

This regex `'^[A-Za-z-]+'` matches leading letters and hyphens. For "CON-00001", this would give "00001". This works correctly based on the output I saw.

For the Account model:
```sql
'001' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(kunden_nr), '[^0-9]', '', 'g'), 15, '0') AS "Id",
```

This uses `[^0-9]` with global flag. For "CUST-1001", this would give "1001". This also works correctly.

For the Opportunity model:
```sql
'006' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(c.chance_id), '[^0-9]', ''), 15, '0') AS "Id",
```

```sql
WITH account_ids AS (
    SELECT 
        TRIM(kunden_nr) AS kunden_nr,
         '001' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(kunden_nr), '^[A-Za-z-]+', ''), 15, '0') AS sf_account_id
    FROM {{ source('fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src', 'kunden') }}
)
```

This uses `'^[A-Za-z-]+'` regex which matches leading letters and hyphens. For "CUST-1001", this would give "1001". Then LPAD('1001', 15, '0') = '000000000001001'. And '001' + that = '0010000000001001'. 

Wait, but in the Account model itself, it uses `[^0-9]` with global flag, which would also give "1001" from "CUST-1001". So both approaches should produce the same result for well-formatted IDs.

But there's a subtle difference: `'^[A-Za-z-]+'` only strips leading alphabetic characters and hyphens. If there were digits within the prefix (unlikely but possible), they would be kept. Whereas `[^0-9]` with global flag strips ALL non-digit characters everywhere. For the given data format "CUST-XXXX", both approaches give the same result, so this isn't a bug per se, just an inconsistency in approach.

However, looking at the Opportunity model again:
```sql
'006' || LPAD(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(c.chance_id), '[^0-9]', ''), 15, '0') AS "Id",
```

This uses `[^0-9]` WITHOUT the global flag. In PostgreSQL, the default behavior of REGEXP_REPLACE without the 'g' flag is to replace only the first match. But since `[^0-9]` matches any single non-digit character, and we're replacing each non-match individually... actually wait, REGEXP_REPLACE without 'g' replaces all occurrences in standard regex semantics (the difference between 'g' and no 'g' is about whether it continues after a replacement).

Actually, I need to clarify: In PostgreSQL, `REGEXP_REPLACE(string, pattern, replacement)` without the 'g' flag will replace only the first occurrence of the pattern. With 'g' flag, it replaces all occurrences.

So for "OPP-00001" with pattern `[^0-9]` and no 'g' flag:
- First match: 'O' at position 1 is replaced with '' -> "PP-00001"
- That's it, because without 'g', only the first occurrence is replaced.

For "OPP-00001" with pattern `[^0-9]` and 'g' flag:
- All non-digit characters are replaced: 'O', 'P', 'P', '-' -> "00001"

That's the bug! The Opportunity model is missing the 'g' flag on REGEXP_REPLACE.

Yes! This confirms the bug. Without the 'g' flag, REGEXP_REPLACE only replaces the first match ('O'), leaving "PP-00001". Then LPAD gives "0000000PP-00001", and prepending '006' gives "0060000000PP-00001".

So the Opportunity model has a clear bug: missing 'g' flag in REGEXP_REPLACE calls.

```sql
REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(c.chance_id), '[^0-9]', '')

to:
